# LLM Productivity Multiplier Research
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

> Dimension 2 of 8 | See [../citations.md](../citations.md) for full source details

## Key Finding

Individual productivity gains range from -19% to +56% depending on task type, developer experience, and context quality. Organizational throughput shows no measurable improvement or slight decline. The perception-reality gap is 39-44 percentage points.

---

## Controlled Experiments

| Study | N | Task Type | Result | Stat. Sig. | Ref |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peng et al. 2023 | 95 | Greenfield JS HTTP server | **+55.8% faster** | p=0.0017 | [1] |
| Cui et al. 2024 | 4,867 | Enterprise tasks (3 companies) | **+26% more completed tasks/week** | Combined only | [2] |
| METR 2025 | 16 | Real issues, mature codebases | **-19% (slower)** | CI: +2% to +39% | [3] |
| Google RCT 2024 | 96 | C++ logging feature | **+21% faster** | Not sig. w/ controls | [4] |
| BIS/Ant Group 2024 | 1,219 | Production coding (CodeFuse) | **+55% LOC** (juniors only sig.) | Significant | [5] |
| Noy & Zhang 2023 | 453 | Writing tasks (not coding) | **+40% faster, +18% quality** | Science | [6] |

---

## Experience-Level Moderation

The most consistent finding across studies:

| Developer Level | Typical Gain | Sources |
|---|---|---|
| Junior / <2 years | +35% to +67% | [2][5][26] |
| Mid-level | +20% to +26% | [2][37] |
| Senior / experienced | +7% to +16% | [2] |
| Expert on familiar codebase | **-19% (slower)** | [3] |

**Contradictory evidence on seniors:** A *Science* (2025) study of 30M+ Python contributions found productivity gains driven exclusively by experienced users [orchestrator research]. Senior developers spend 4.3 min reviewing each AI suggestion vs. 1.2 min for juniors [orchestrator research].

---

## Task-Type Variation

| Task Type | AI Impact | Evidence Quality |
|---|---|---|
| Boilerplate / repetitive | +20% to +50% | Moderate (practitioner consensus) |
| Greenfield / simple | +21% to +56% | Strong (RCTs [1][4]) |
| Test generation | +80% execution timeline reduction | Vendor claims [36] |
| Documentation | +7.5% quality, +10-15% time savings | Moderate [21] |
| Architecture / design | ~0% (negligible) | Weak (surveys [24]) |
| Debugging existing code | Negative to negligible | Weak [3][24] |
| Code review | -31.8% cycle time | One longitudinal study |

---

## Organizational-Level Impact

| Metric | Change | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Individual tasks completed | +21% | [27] |
| PRs merged | +98% | [27] |
| PR size increase | +154% | [27] |
| PR review time increase | +91% | [27] |
| Bugs per developer | +9% | [27] |
| DORA delivery throughput | -1.5% per 25% AI adoption | [21] |
| DORA delivery stability | -7.2% per 25% AI adoption | [21] |
| System-level productivity ceiling | ~10% | Six studies converge [27] |
| Incidents per PR | +23.5% | [29] |
| Change failure rate | +30% | [29] |

**The Bain Correction [38]:** Coding + testing = 25-35% of SDLC. A 30% coding speedup yields only ~9% total delivery improvement:
```
Total AI Impact = 1 + (AI_Coding_Speedup - 1) x SDLC_Coding_Share
Example: 1 + (1.30 - 1) x 0.30 = 1.09 (9% improvement)
```

---

## Self-Reported vs. Measured

| Source | Self-Reported | Measured | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| METR RCT [3] | +20% to +24% faster | -19% (slower) | **39-44 pp** |
| DX Survey [26] | 3.6-4.4 hrs/week saved | Not measured | Unknown |
| Anthropic Internal [83] | +50% productivity | +67% PRs/day | Partially validated |
| DORA [21][22] | 75%+ feel more productive | -1.5% throughput | Significant |

---

## Code Quality Trade-offs

| Metric | Change | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Code churn (revised within 2 weeks) | 3.1% -> 5.7% (+84%) | [23] |
| Copy/pasted code | 8.3% -> 12.3% (+48%) | [23] |
| Refactoring share | 25% -> <10% (-60%) | [23] |
| Duplicated code blocks | 8x increase | [23] |
| AI PR issues vs human PR issues | 1.7x more | [31] |
| AI code security failures | 45% fail | [32] |
| AI code vulnerabilities | 2.74x more than human | [32] |

---

## Synthesis: Realistic Multiplier Ranges

For estimation purposes, conservative multipliers based on the evidence:

| Context | Multiplier | Confidence |
|---|---|---|
| Junior dev, routine/greenfield task | 1.3x - 1.5x | Moderate |
| Mid-level dev, mixed tasks | 1.1x - 1.3x | Moderate |
| Senior dev, familiar codebase | 1.0x (no adjustment) | High (METR) |
| Any dev, unfamiliar/greenfield | 1.2x - 1.4x | Low-Moderate |
| System-level delivery impact | 1.0x - 1.1x | High (DORA, Faros) |

**Apply the Bain Correction:** Multiply coding-phase gains by SDLC coding share (0.25-0.35) to get total delivery impact.

## Gaps and Limitations

- No controlled study specifically measures Cursor or Claude Code productivity (METR is closest but used early-2025 tools) [3]
- Most RCTs test simple/greenfield tasks; no RCT tests complex enterprise integration work
- Vendor-funded studies (GitHub [1], AWS [40], Ant Group [5]) show 2-3x larger effects than independent studies (METR [3], Google [4])
- The "right" multiplier changes as tools improve -- any framework must include recalibration cadence
